title: Northern Farmer (New Style)
content:
I.
DOSNïŋ―T thou ïŋ―ear my ïŋ―erseïŋ―s legs, as they canters awaïŋ―y?
Proputty, proputty, proputtyïŋ―thatïŋ―s what I ïŋ―ears ïŋ―em saïŋ―y.
Proputty, proputty, proputtyïŋ―Sam, thouïŋ―s an ass for thy paaïŋ―ns:
Theerïŋ―s moor sense iïŋ― one oïŋ― ïŋ―is legs nor in all thy braïŋ―ins.

II.
Woïŋ―theerïŋ―s a craw to pluck wiïŋ― tha, Sam: yonïŋ―s parsonïŋ―s ïŋ―ouseïŋ―
Dosnïŋ―t thou knaw that a man mun be eïŋ―ther a man or a mouse?
Time to think on it then; for thouïŋ―ll be twenty to weeïŋ―k.
Proputty, proputtyïŋ―woïŋ― then, woïŋ―let ma ïŋ―ear mysïŋ―n speïŋ―k.

III.
Me anïŋ― thy muther, Sammy, ïŋ―as beïŋ―n a-talkinïŋ― oïŋ― thee;
Thouïŋ―s beïŋ―n talkinïŋ― to muther, anïŋ― she beïŋ―n a tellinïŋ― it me.
Thouïŋ―ll not marry for munnyïŋ―thouïŋ―s sweet upoïŋ― parsonïŋ―s lassïŋ―
Noïŋ―thouïŋ―ll marry fur luvvïŋ―anïŋ― we boïŋ―th on us thinks tha an ass.

IV.
Seeïŋ―d her todaïŋ―y goïŋ― byïŋ―Saïŋ―intïŋ―s-daïŋ―yïŋ―they was ringing the bells.
Sheïŋ―s a beauty thou thinksïŋ―anïŋ― soïŋ― is scoors oïŋ― gells,
Them as ïŋ―as munny anïŋ― allïŋ―wotïŋ―s a beauty?ïŋ―the flower as blaws.
But proputty, proputty sticks, anïŋ― proputty, proputty graws.

V.
Doïŋ―ant be stunt: taïŋ―k time: I knaws what maïŋ―kes tha sa mad.
Warnïŋ―t I craïŋ―zed fur the lasses mysïŋ―n when I wur a lad?
But I knawed a Quaïŋ―ker feller as often ïŋ―as towd ma this:
ïŋ―Doïŋ―nt thou marry for munny, but goïŋ― wheer munny is!ïŋ―

VI.
Anïŋ― I went wheer munny war: anïŋ― thy muther coom to ïŋ―and,
Wiïŋ― lots oïŋ― munny laaïŋ―d by, anïŋ― a nicetish bit oïŋ― land.
Maïŋ―ybe she warnïŋ―t a beauty:ïŋ―I niver giv it a thowtïŋ―
But warnïŋ―t she as good to cuddle anïŋ― kiss as a lass as ïŋ―ant nowt?

VII.
Parsonïŋ―s lass ïŋ―ant nowt, anïŋ― she weïŋ―nt ïŋ―a nowt when ïŋ―eïŋ―s deïŋ―d,
Mun be a guvness, lad, or summut, and addle her breïŋ―d:
Why? fur ïŋ―eïŋ―s nobbut a curate, anïŋ― weïŋ―nt niver git hissen clear,
Anïŋ― ïŋ―e maïŋ―de the bed as ïŋ―e ligs on afoor ïŋ―e coomed to the shere.

VIII.
An thin ïŋ―e coomïŋ―d to the parish wiïŋ― lots oïŋ― Varsity debt,
Stook to his taaïŋ―l they did, anïŋ― ïŋ―e ïŋ―ant got shut on ïŋ―em yet.
Anïŋ― ïŋ―e ligs on ïŋ―is back iïŋ― the grip, wiïŋ― noïŋ―n to lend ïŋ―im a shovv,
Woorse nor a far-welterïŋ―d yowe: fur, Sammy, ïŋ―e married fur luvv.

IX.
Luvv? whatïŋ―s luvv? thou can luvv thy lass anïŋ― ïŋ―er munny too,
Maïŋ―kinïŋ― ïŋ―em goaïŋ― togither as theyïŋ―ve good right to do.
Couldïŋ―n I luvv thy muther by cause oïŋ― ïŋ―er munny laaïŋ―d by?
Naïŋ―yïŋ―fur I luvvïŋ―d ïŋ―er a vast sight moor fur it: reïŋ―son why.

X.
Ay anïŋ― thy muther says thou wants to marry the lass,
Cooms of a gentleman burn: anïŋ― we boïŋ―th on us thinks tha an ass.
Woïŋ― then, proputty, wiltha?ïŋ―an ass as near as mays nowtïŋ―
Woïŋ― then, wiltha? dangtha!ïŋ―the bees is as fell as owt.

XI.
Breïŋ―k me a bit oïŋ― the esh for his ïŋ―eïŋ―d, lad, out oïŋ― the fence!
Gentleman burn! whatïŋ―s gentleman burn? is it shillins anïŋ― pence?
Proputty, proputtyïŋ―s ivrything ïŋ―ere, anïŋ―, Sammy, Iïŋ―m blest
If it isnïŋ―t the saïŋ―me oop yonder, fur them as ïŋ―as itïŋ―s the best.

XII.
Tisïŋ―n them as ïŋ―as munny as breaks into ïŋ―ouses anïŋ― steïŋ―ls,
Them as ïŋ―as coïŋ―ts to their backs anïŋ― taïŋ―kes their regular meïŋ―ls.
Noïŋ―, but itïŋ―s them as niver knaws wheer a meïŋ―lïŋ―s to be ïŋ―ad.
Taïŋ―ke my word for it, Sammy, the poor in a loomp is bad.

XIII.
Them or thir feythers, tha sees, mun ïŋ―a beïŋ―n a laïŋ―zy lot,
Fur work mun ïŋ―a gone to the gittinïŋ― whiniver munny was got.
Feyther ïŋ―ad ammost nowt; leïŋ―stways ïŋ―is munny was ïŋ―id.
But ïŋ―e tued anïŋ― moilïŋ―d ïŋ―issïŋ―n deïŋ―d, an ïŋ―e died a good un, ïŋ―e did.

XIV.
Loook thou theer wheer Wrigglesby beck cooms out by the ïŋ―ill!
Feyther run oop to the farm, anïŋ― I runs oop to the mill;
Anïŋ― Iïŋ―ll run oop to the brig, anïŋ― that thouïŋ―ll live to see;
And if thou marries a good un Iïŋ―ll leïŋ―ve the land to thee.

XV.
Thimïŋ―s my noïŋ―tions, Sammy, wheerby I means to stick;
But if thou marries a bad un, Iïŋ―ll leïŋ―ve the land to Dick.ïŋ―
Coom oop, proputty, proputtyïŋ―thatïŋ―s what I ïŋ―ears ïŋ―im saïŋ―yïŋ―
Proputty, proputty, proputtyïŋ―canter anïŋ― canter awaïŋ―y.
meaning:
"Northern Farmer (New Style)" is a quintessential example of Alfred Lord Tennyson's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
